schools.summary: A supreme Philistine effort strikes the heart of the land at Jezreel; Ellicott sees David reaping his own error, Keil the covenant God about to deliver him.
Had Israel and Saul not forsaken God there would be no Philistines to molest them; David's ambiguous answer keeps him free of a promise.
Ellicott: David temporises, dares not refuse; Achish, won by his character, makes him captain of the guard, though the Philistine lords are not fooled.
Gill: David is undetermined, trusting Providence to extricate him rather than committing to either side.
Clarke and Wesley: a deliberately equivocal answer, to be read only by David's later conduct.

debates.question: What does David mean by 'what thy servant can do'?

sides.position: a studied ambiguity, trusting God to deliver him from serving against Israel
Achish takes it, wrongly, as a firm engagement to fight for the Philistines

schools.summary: The repeated notice of Samuel's death introduces the story; Ellicott parses ôb ('hollow voice'/ventriloquist) and 'wizards' ('wise people,' ironic).
Clarke: with Samuel dead there is no accredited prophet; the edict rests on the Mosaic law against witches.
Topography: Philistines at Shunem, Saul opposite on Mount Gilboa, the hosts in full view across the valley; Shunem recalls Abishag and the Shunammite.
Henry: the enemy in Issachar's territory shows the land ill-guarded while Saul wore out his strength pursuing David.
The real cause of Saul's terror is not the enemy's numbers but the consciousness that God had forsaken him; Ellicott pictures him gazing at the Philistine tents.
Wesley: had he kept close to God he need not have feared all the armies of the Philistines.
Henry: a guilty conscience recalls the spared Amalekites and the slain priests, making him tremble at the shaking of a leaf.
The three appointed media — dreams, Urim, prophets — are explained; Ellicott asks how Saul could use the Urim at all, the ephod being with David since Nob.
Gill: the inquiry was not made in faith but with a double mind, so that it was as if he had not inquired (1 Chron. 10:13-14).
Henry: Saul had forfeited every channel of inquiry by persecuting prophets, slaying the priest, and grieving the Spirit.
Henry: rebuffed by heaven, Saul 'knocks at the gates of hell,' guilty both of contempt of God and of self-contradiction, consulting what he had outlawed.
Keil locates the baalath-ob at En-dor near Little Hermon; Ellicott sees his last spark of zeal turned to desperate superstition ('Acheronta movebo').
Keil: divining by the ôb meant calling a spirit up from Sheol; Ellicott dwells on the popular geography of Sheol 'below' (Eph. 4:9) and the fascination of the black arts.
Wesley and Clarke: the disguise sprang from shame and from fear the woman would not ply her art before the king who had proscribed it.
Gill: Saul stripped off his royal robes to pass from the camp unrecognised as well as to deceive the woman.
The woman fears a snare under Saul's own capital law (Lev. 20:27); Ellicott allows she may enhance the peril to extort a larger fee.
Henry: Providence makes Saul hear his own edict to his face; she fears man's punishment more than God's, dreading a snare for her life over one for her soul.
Gill marvels that Saul could take the LORD's name into his mouth while doing such a work of darkness, forgetting the omniscience he swore by.
Keil: the oath 'showed how utterly hardened Saul was' — pledging God's name to shield a crime punishable by death.
Ellicott gathers the rabbinic lore (the soul near the body twelve months) and the pathos: Saul yearns for the friend of his youth, quoting Trench.
Clarke: the witch meant only to have her familiar personate the named person; fallen spirits' knowledge is limited. Wesley: Saul sought Samuel's old compassion.
Ellicott: the woman saw what she never dreamed of; how she knew Saul is weighed (Josephus: Samuel revealed it; Keil: clairvoyance; Ewald: threatening gestures).
Clarke and Wesley: Samuel really appeared, raised neither by devil nor witch, her familiar not coming — the surprise proving her art had no part in it.
Gill: her very terror proves this was not the true Samuel but a permitted apparition.

debates.question: Who or what actually appeared at En-dor?

sides.position: the true Samuel, brought up by God's overruling and beyond the witch's power
not the true Samuel but a spirit/apparition permitted to deceive

schools.summary: The plural elohim is read as one majestic 'celestial being' (Keil; à Lapide), though antiquity (Talmud Chaggigah) and the Chaldee ('an angel') saw more, or a robed 'judge.'
Clarke: elohim is the word for the Supreme Being, and the unexpected sight alarmed the woman; Wesley: 'a god, a divine person,' plural by Hebrew idiom.

debates.question: Does 'gods' (elohim) mean one form or more?

sides.position: more than one form — Samuel and another (a 'judge')

schools.summary: The mantle (m'il) Samuel wore in life (1 Sam. 15:27) is the token of recognition; Ellicott answers with Maimonides and Wordsworth how a spirit can be seen and clothed.
Wesley: if it were not Samuel but a spirit in his shape, then it is not true that Saul 'perceived it was Samuel'; Clarke reads two staged appearances.
Henry (with Bishop Patrick): a witch cannot disturb the rest of the godly, so this is an evil spirit personating Samuel to rivet Saul in the devil's interest.
Keil leaves undecided whether witch or God disturbed Samuel (Isa. 14:9); Ellicott hears no confession of sin, and traces Saul's omission of the Urim to shame over Nob.
Clarke: the reproach falls on Saul, not the woman, whose incantations had no influence; Wesley mourns that he called Samuel too late.

debates.question: Is the speaker the real Samuel or a demon in his likeness?

sides.position: the true Samuel, or a spirit constrained by God, disturbed from Hades

schools.summary: Keil ties the reproach to the old sentence of rejection (1 Sam. 15:23); Ellicott weighs the rare word 'enemy' (Ps. 139:20) and the LXX/Vulgate 'with thy neighbours.'
Clarke hears the prophet's true accent — 'it is his counsel alone that I communicate' — an argument for a real Samuel.
Gill: the devil, personating Samuel, is here made to reason out a terrible truth.
Clarke: 'as he spake by me — none but Samuel could say this'; he also prefers the reading 'the LORD hath done to thee' from several manuscripts.
Ellicott, with Wordsworth: an evil spirit would not help David to the throne nor honour God's justice, so the true Samuel speaks; margin reads 'done for himself.'
Gill: even the deceiving spirit 'sometimes betrays himself' by being made to speak the truth about David.

debates.question: Does the prophecy of David's kingdom prove the speaker is really Samuel?

sides.position: yes — only the true prophet (or a spirit constrained by God) would advance David and honour divine justice
no — a deceiving spirit is here compelled to utter the truth

themes: kingdom rent and given to David

schools.summary: Gill refers the sentence back to the disobedience over Amalek and (with Abarbinel) the earlier failure at Gilgal, when the kingdom was first threatened.
Henry: the spirit now aggravates the very sin it once helped Saul excuse, quoting divine truth for a malicious end, to drive him to despair.
Ellicott counts three judgments — defeat, the death of Saul and his sons, the sack of the camp; 'to morrow' (machar) can mean the near future; Keil notes Saul's collapse.
Clarke and Wesley read 'thou and thy sons with me' hopefully (Charles Wesley's verses): a 'sin unto death,' yet mercy possibly to the soul.
Augustine (via Wordsworth): 'with me' means a like condition of death, not an equality in bliss — a gentle word drawing Saul toward repentance.
Gill: a foul spirit bent on despair, offering no comfort or call to repentance, only ruin.

debates.question: Does 'thou and thy sons shall be with me' hold out hope of Saul's salvation?

sides.position: a ray of hope — repose with Samuel, mercy to the soul though death to the body
merely 'thou shalt be as I am, among the dead' — a like state of death, not equality in bliss

themes: tomorrow thou shalt be with me

schools.summary: Saul falls his full length, weakened by a day's fast and the rough night walk, swooning under the message (Clarke, Ellicott, Keil).
Henry: he asked what he should do and was told only what he had not done; terrified to despair, he was 'not humbled' — no confession, sacrifice, or supplication.
Wesley: his past sins were surely brought to remembrance — who dare affirm he felt no contrition, though it is not recorded?

debates.question: Was there any contrition in Saul's collapse?

sides.position: none — he was terrified but not humbled, rushing on his doom
possibly — his sins came to remembrance; the text is silent, not decisive

schools.summary: Ellicott: the witch's woman's pity for the stricken king contradicts the theory that hatred drove her to feign Samuel by ventriloquism; Keil corrects the 'other room' assumption.
Wesley: she returned from the place to which she had withdrawn while Saul and Samuel conversed.
Gill sets out her reasonable plea: as she hearkened to him at peril of her life, he should hearken to her for his own preservation.
Keil: servants and woman compelled him from natural sympathy, not to allay suspicion, 'for she had not deceived the king at all.'
Henry finds a mournful fitness in a king reduced to so wretched a comforter, coaxed to eat by an outlawed woman.
Keil: Saul, compelled, sits on the mittah (cushioned bench); Clarke and Ellicott note the 'bed' is a divan and deny any warrant for the popular 'cave.'
Gill: he refused either choosing famine over the sword or unable to eat, his stomach loathing food in his grief.
Henry: a rational, courteous 'compelling' (Luke 14:23; Job 6:25); yet 'what are dainty meats to a wounded conscience?'
Clarke explains the Eastern dispatch in cookery — young animals kept ready — and the unleavened bread baked for lack of time (so also Wesley).
Gill: the fat calf was a choice dish; Josephus commends the woman's liberality as worthy of imitation.
Keil: her whole service flowed from natural sympathy for the unhappy king.
Clarke sums the whole in articles: no incantation has power over a saint, yet 'Samuel did actually appear,' sent by God's mercy to warn Saul, the oracle being beyond human or diabolical foresight.
Ellicott and Keil (with von Gerlach): Saul, too hardened for grief, goes 'in stolid desperation' to meet his fate — the terrible end of a man once turned into another man by the Spirit.
Henry: he steals away before light from a scandalous house, and marvels less at his courage than at a heart that would not turn to God in repentance.

debates.question: Was the whole episode a real appearance of Samuel by God's power, or a hardened man's self-ruin?

sides.position: a genuine, God-sent appearance to warn the doomed king
the tragic close of Saul's self-hardening, read morally rather than metaphysically

themes: Clarke's defence of a real Samuel
